text: INDITEX GENERAL E. ENVIRONMENTAL S. SOCIAL G. GOVERNANCE ANNEXES INDEPENDENT AT A GLANCE INFORMATION INFORMATION INFORMATION INFORMATION VERIFICATION REPORT Transition risks E1.IRO-1_10; E1.IRO-1_11; E1.IRO-1_12; E1.SBM-3_01; E1.SBM-3_06 Transition risks vary significantly depending on the level of ambition of each pathway analysed, and they may affect different areas of our business. To gauge their potential impact, we have assessed various dimensions of transition risks in the short, medium and long term: Regulatory, Legal liability, Technological, Market and Reputation. /Transition risk: envisages establishing an explicit carbon price as a possible measure for governments to incentivise the transition to a low-carbon economy. /Legal liability: considers developments in connection with climaterelated litigation. /Technological: analyses how the development and use of new technologies that are less GHG-intensive would affect the Group. /Market: envisages an increasing market interest in sustainable products and services. /Reputation: considers a context with no progress towards a lowcarbon economy, valuing the effects of public opinion, climate activism and investors’ perceptions. The level of uncertainty associated with analysing the impacts of climate change is particularly significant in the case of transition risk, because the underlying assumptions of each scenario are based on projections made at a given point in time about how future events may evolve, such as legislation in different countries, technological innovations, consumer preferences, and the extent of litigation on sustainability issues, for example. These assumptions are inherently volatile, as they depend on external developments. Furthermore, there is often insufficiently comprehensive or robust historical data available for application to the textile sector.
notes: Risk assessment narrative — describes uncertainty rather than making a claim.

By 2020, our leading fabric suppliers had eliminated new labor fees in our supply chain—a meaningful change we helped bring about that’s now saving approximately 3,000 workers in our supply chain a total of $1.7 million per year. Forced and bonded labor was identified as another material topic in our Double Materiality Assessment (page 11), and we’re now working on a large-scale effort with a coalition of other brands to seek reimbursement of recruitment fees paid by workers prior to 2020 and to push for legislative solutions. In 2022, we conducted a survey of over 1,600 migrant workers in Thailand and Taiwan; only 3% of workers owed money in labor fees and, in both countries, a large majority were hoping to renew their contracts. Through our annual audits, we continue to seek feedback from workers on their experiences and validate that they do not pay fees. “We’ve worked to improve conditions supplier by supplier,” Nguyen says, “But that won’t change the whole industry. We need legislative support. Since labor fees are legal in these Employees at the Sheico wetsuit factory in Thailand. countries, it’s important to keep pushing for local laws that Ryan ‘Chachi’ Craig say no more fees, period.” Our work on migrant worker fees helped save 3,000 workers an annual average of $1.7 M in recruitment fees Intro Business Unusual Product Grants & Giving Activism Work in Progress Report 77 Our Pollution Problem In FY2025, we focused on eliminating “forever chemicals,” reducing our carbon emissions, and cutting the textile waste our products create— whether that’s when they’re made or at the product’s end-of-life. Josué Rivas Intro Business Unusual Product Grants & Giving Activism Work in Progress Report 38 On the insurance front, we reevaluate policies every year and prioritize carriers with fossil fuel exclusions and decarbonization commitments. We’ve replicated our banking criteria with our corporate insurance purchasing process, where we now require our insurance companies to submit sustainability policies and targets with their annual bids for Patagonia’s business. We meet annually to review progress and push for more ambitious targets. In 2019, we added a new 401(k) option called The Cleanest Line Fund, so employees can choose to invest their retirement savings in ways that match their environmental values. In 2024, we started a new finance program to help our finished goods suppliers get paid faster on Patagonia invoices. It offers better terms to factories that do well with environmental practices, worker treatment and product quality, and encourages other supplier partners to improve. These steps reflect a broader shift in how we think about money—not just as a tool for business, but as a way to support people and the planet at every level of our operations. For anyone asking how to start this process, we can say this: Begin with one account.

Mechi Fahs Intro Business Unusual Product Grants & Giving Activism Work in Progress Report 25 B-ing in Business for the Planet We became a Certified B Corp in 2011 and California’s first legal benefit corporation in 2012—on the first date California law allowed it. In doing so, we adopted six legal commitments that guide how we operate: Donating 1% of sales to environmental groups Building the best product with no unnecessary harm Conducting our operations without unnecessary harm Sharing best practices with other businesses Operating with transparency Providing a just, equitable and supportive work environment To certify, we selected B Lab as the third-party assessor. When we became a benefit corporation, we formally wrote into our corporate DNA that we would prioritize the planet before profit—an early precursor to our new corporate structure. Recertification happens every three years through B Lab’s B Impact Assessment, which measures a company’s impact on workers, community, environment, customers and our governance practices. Since 2013, our B Corp score has increased year over year, from 107.3 to now 166 out of roughly 250 (the median score is 50.9). Since 2013, we also have published an annual B Corp report on our website (check out our past reports here). In 2016, we began closing all US locations on Election Day—a first in company history—so all employees could make it to the polls. In 2024, we shifted that closure to national Vote Early Day.

text: PRINCIPLE: DIGITAL ETHICS Lush Digital believes technology should give more than it takes from society and the environment. We champion ethical hardware, ethical data, ethical design, and open source technologies and we support and elevate communities who feel the same. Just as Lush believes in ethically sourced ingredients, our Digital business believes in ethical tech that gives back to the community. To keep us accountable to the beliefs that we hold true as a technological business, Lush Digital formulated its own set of Digital Ethics Policies to consider every time we design, build or launch a digital product. They can be consolidated into three categories: Open Source, Ethical Hardware and Ethical Data. Our open source policy aims to equally contribute to open-source tech communities, wherever we can, in order to support the grassroots world of creating tech for everyone to share and benefit from without the heavy burden of cost. We are committed to the principles of open source and in that spirit, we strive to use technologies with OSI-approved licenses and to contribute our research and code back to open communities under such licenses. Our Ethical Hardware policy guides us to choose the best supplier we can in terms of their approach to sourcing materials, reparability, energy consumption and production methods used. Our Ethical Data policy is about ensuring that all of Lush’s staff and customer data is secure and transparent as our customers and staff have the right to know what we hold about them. It states that we will ensure all data that is stored on our products or systems is encrypted and secure, that we will ensure your data can be accessed by you only and those you give permission to and that we are transparent about how we use customer and staff data.
notes: Digital ethics policies — not environmental claims.

text: We will continue to publicly campaign on a variety of issues and press for a kinder, fairer society, so that all of us can pay tax into a system we can feel proud of, and which supports everyone in society equally. We have been accredited by the Fair Tax Foundation since 2015 and were the first high street multinational company in the UK to be awarded the Fair Tax Mark. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 17 Principle: Lush is a Campaigning Company Core Principle: Lush actively campaigns on the issues of human rights, animal protection and environmental justice. All Lush licensed territories are expected to campaign at least once a year on one of the core values through their shops and website. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 18 PRINCIPLE: LUSH IS A CAMPAIGNING COMPANY Since the beginning, Lush has collaborated with various organizations to run a number of campaigns in-store and online, focusing our efforts on helping people, animals and the planet and fighting for human rights, animal protection and environmental justice. We have been campaigning online and in our shop windows since 2006 and have covered a plethora of issues. In FY2024, we ran a total of 12 campaigns globally. Two were global campaigns, four in the UK & Ireland, four in Europe and two in North America.

Imagine if we could eliminate the need for packaging altogether? This is the challenge we pose to our Inventors and R&D teams, whose legacy has given us the shampoo bar, massage bar, naked deodorant, henna brick, naked body conditioner, naked shower gel and more soap than you could ever dream of. Naked products also tend to last longer than their packaged counterparts because they are more concentrated and have minimal water content. Tests with users tell us that one shampoo bar can last the equivalent washes as three 200ml bottles of liquid shampoo. Not only are we saving those three bottles worth of plastic, we are also having to ship less product and less weight, reducing C02 emissions. The full report will take you through how we arrived at these estimations. Our initial interest in making naked products was born out of necessity. After the demise of Cosmetics To Go, the Lush Co-Founders simply didn’t have the money to invest in packaging, but this actually sparked a revolutionary approach to a growing industry problem. We have been selling solid products even in the years before Lush, when we were known as Cosmetics To Go. However, they were not born naked!

Principle: Recycled Materials Evolving Principle: If we can’t make everything naked, then the next aim is to ensure that those products that do need packaging use the best and most innovative minimum impact, recycled or reused materials that are available. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 67 PRINCIPLE: RECYCLED MATERIALS When packaging is absolutely necessary, we make it count. We've been using recycled plastic since 2008 in the UK. We recently switched the colour additives from virgin to recycled plastic for black pots in the UK and are rolling this out to other markets, so we can claim 100% recycled pots. Most of our pesky pumps, virgin until recently, now have 30-60% recycled content. Perfume spritzers, Shield & Willow and the 100ml Super Milk are still a work in progress. For our 100% recycled PET bottles, we went further and sourced Prevented Ocean Plastic for most markets (not in North America yet). This comes from bottle collectors working 50km from water bodies, helping prevent bottles reaching the ocean. Labels, despite our best efforts, remain unfortunately virgin.Our gifts make up for it with recycled paper, cotton rags, agriculture waste like banana fibre, knot-wraps from recycled bottles, and ribbons from plastic bottles in use.

It was essential that we work with the LGBTQIA+ Community Network and the Trans community more specifically when creating this policy to ensure it truly reflected their lived experiences, needs and priorities. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 30 PRINCIPLE: ALL ARE WELCOME, ALWAYS Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 31 Principle: Commitment to Transparency and External Audits Core Principle: Lush will contract regular third party audits of its Animal Testing Policy, its ethical and environmental practices as well as regular business audits. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 32 PRINCIPLE: COMMITMENT TO TRANSPARENCY AND EXTERNAL AUDITS If you're going to make bold claims about your ethics, you'd better be prepared to prove them. What’s more, having an eye from outside the business can shed light on practices that need to be improved. We are also finding that many audits are now becoming legal requirements, like they should be! There are a couple of external audits that we commission throughout our business, as well as some internal audits we conduct ourselves to measure our own progress. One of the biggest external audits we commission is the yearly audit of our Non-Animal Testing Policy.

To align to natural cycles, any materials we no longer use should be repurposed, recycled or composted so they can keep generating value. In FY24 we estimate that we generated 7,464 tonnes of total waste globally, improving to 63% diversion rate globally in FY24. This is an increase on the 6,226 tonnes generated in FY22, mainly due to increase in production and sales. Manufacturing globally has a 71% diversion rate. Germany manufacturing had the highest diversion rate in the Lush manufacturing world at 88%, closely followed by North America manufacturing at 82%. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 76 PRINCIPLE: ENVIRONMENTAL POLICIES Retail sits at 54% on average, with many shops around the world without access to adequate facilities for recycling materials - in North America we have been working with shopping malls to improve access. Wet paper towels are the single biggest source of non-recyclable waste for our shops! We ask our shops to ‘weigh the waste’ once a year so we can collect the data and they can get more familiar with what goes on in their waste. When it comes to product waste, if the product is still usable we make every effort to donate it.

The policy aims to guide our supplier’s buying practices to ensure they align with Lush’s company values and underpin our commitment to doing business in the most ethical way that we can. Our buying team, and the wider business, have multiple resources to help suppliers with any barriers they may be facing, or improvements they are passionate about. Our relationships are always mutually beneficial and we know that there is so much to learn from communities who have made these ingredients their livelihoods. One source of support we offer our suppliers is through the Improvements & Remediation Fund. This is a discretionary fund, dedicated to supporting engaged suppliers in projects that improve their social or environmental impact, or further our mission to fight animal testing. Suppliers who have shown an interest in making improvements to social and environmental standards, or are otherwise heavily engaged with Lush’s values are invited by our creative buying team to apply for funding on projects that meet our criteria. In FY2024, we carried out 13 projects in collaboration with our suppliers, in which we contributed about £166,000 Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 45 PRINCIPLE: FAIR BUSINESS DEALINGS WITH OUR SUPPLIERS Despite Fairtrade certification and other certification schemes being a useful tool, and a reassuring mark of producers and traders doing the right thing, Lush as a company does not solely rely on these certifications. We find that working as directly as possible with primary producers of our ingredients and packaging is the best way for us to achieve transparency in our supply network, while also making sure that our supplier relationships are fair and built on open communication. The term fair trade, whilst associated with formal certifications, can be expressed and verified in many forms, some of which are far more economically and structurally viable for our producers.

Since 2010 we have been gradually replacing palm-derived materials. We increased transparency with our suppliers and better monitoring through GPS combined with satellite data. In FY24, we reduced the use of palm-derived ingredients by 10% as we moved to sodium coco-sulfate as the base of our shampoo bars. 46% of raw materials by weight that would normally be derived from palm have been sourced from a diversity of different plant oil feedstocks. Ingredients like Tonka Beans, Illipe Butter or Brazil Nut oil help forest peoples to get economic value from a standing forest. Long-term partnerships and additional funding meant 138 families connected to Murumuru Butter benefited from a tree planting programme, with Guyra Paraguay NGO, 20 hectares of agroforestry were implemented, protecting 6,900 hectares of the Atlantic Rainforest and providing livelihoods for more than 50 families. Through our land defenders fund, £200,000 donated to those protecting ecosystems with their lives. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 40 PRINCIPLE: A LIVING PLANET Rewilding Life 17 ingredients directly benefit wildlife Through partnerships with conservation organisations, local communities and even our own forest concessions, we source ingredients helping to protect wildlife. In Zambia, COMACO works with illegal wildlife poachers to transition them to bee-keeping and support small-holder farmers with training to improve livelihoods.

An in-depth review of that company’s completed non-animal testing declaration will be conducted with the supplier themselves, either virtually or in person during a site visit. The auditors can then go through any difficulties or questions the supply company has as well as confirm and verify the declaration is full and accurate. Once this is complete, Ethical Consumer will determine whether the supplier's compliance status needs to change, or can stay the same. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 33 PRINCIPLE: COMMITMENT TO TRANSPARENCY AND EXTERNAL AUDITS Every year, in line with legislation, our Group financial statements are audited by an external company. Recently, this has also included environmental reporting in line with the Taskforce on Climate Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) framework. Our Group financial statements are fully audited by a reputable and qualified audit firm which includes consideration of our TCFD disclosures and Group wide internal controls. Our Group financial statements were signed on 27th March 2024. We also audit some of our buying and supply network practices internally to ensure better visibility across these networks and help, where we can, remediate on any issues we find. Our buying and support teams utilise a piece of software called Authenticate to register our suppliers and start mapping out their networks, back to the first source.

Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 76 PRINCIPLE: ENVIRONMENTAL POLICIES Retail sits at 54% on average, with many shops around the world without access to adequate facilities for recycling materials - in North America we have been working with shopping malls to improve access. Wet paper towels are the single biggest source of non-recyclable waste for our shops! We ask our shops to ‘weigh the waste’ once a year so we can collect the data and they can get more familiar with what goes on in their waste. When it comes to product waste, if the product is still usable we make every effort to donate it. We donate straight from the store or from our manufacturing centres to various charities. If it’s not possible to donate the product then we send these to composting where we can. In the UK, we invested in our own recycling centre, the Green Hub. It processes the Bring it Back returns from the stores and our UK manufacturing wastes. Green Hub: 81% of solid waste recycled, recovered, or composted.

We're almost fossil gas-free in retail and gradually electrifying factories. Our North American sites run on 100% renewable energy despite still utilising gas, by supporting recovery of natural gas from a landfill project. In the UK,our main R&D building (Unit 1 Witney) and our EC’s/Perfumes factory operate fully on electricity with Unit 1 also being supplied by 126kWp of solar PV directly from the roof, supplying over 30% of our annual electricity demand. Our Japanese manufacturing facility is already 81% electrified. We are currently performing energy audits in Japan, LED upgrades across UK facilities, remaining non-LED German shops, better monitoring, double doors in the new Glasgow store to improve efficiency, and ‘power down’ campaigns for staff. Next we want to work on more markets moving to deep green tariffs, investments in further reduction of fossil fuel use in Japan, and Taiwan-specific renewable certificates instead of Chinese RECs. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 64 PRINCIPLE: RENEWABLE ENERGY We're 41% biogas, 59% fossil gas for heating, with our gas-to-electricity ratio at about 30:70. The full report will take you through how we arrived at these estimations. Our initial interest in making naked products was born out of necessity. After the demise of Cosmetics To Go, the Lush Co-Founders simply didn’t have the money to invest in packaging, but this actually sparked a revolutionary approach to a growing industry problem. We have been selling solid products even in the years before Lush, when we were known as Cosmetics To Go. However, they were not born naked! The mail order company would wrap exciting bath bombs, bubble bars, bath oils, soaps, massage bars and the now famous shampoo bar in sometimes extravagant packaging! It was only when Lush opened its first store – in Poole High Street with a delicatessen vibe – that those products went naked. Our latest invention is the Naked Mascara, formulated with butters and waxes to create an iconic, solid product. This has become a favourite product for our customers and has won multiple awards such as the 2022 UK Sustainable Beauty Awards for Sustainable Product, the 2023 NA Harper’s Bazaar Beauty Icons Awards for Most Innovative Beauty Product and the 2024 Australia Sustainability Awards for the Product Award. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 56

Co Create is an initiative that works to bring new minds, voices and cultures into the product creation process. People within the company are given the opportunity to come together and design product ranges that represent their cultural or religious celebrations, with the aim to connect to more customers. Co-Create have worked on many ranges since the project's inception 3 years ago, representing celebrations and holidays that include Eid al-Fitr, Eid al-Adha, Hanukkah, Día De Muertos, Diwali and St Patrick's Day, with more coming soon! We also recently launched our Gender Affirming Care At Work Policy which is live in the Netherlands, the UK and Ireland, with a hope to launch in more markets soon. This policy allows our trans and non binary colleagues peace of mind by providing a clear policy on work place support, including name and pronoun changes, transitioning leave and access to facilities. It was essential that we work with the LGBTQIA+ Community Network and the Trans community more specifically when creating this policy to ensure it truly reflected their lived experiences, needs and priorities. Through a mix of direct ‘deep green’ tariffs — 40% of properties — and standard electricity tariffs with matching impact Renewable Energy Certificate purchases, many with additional benefits such as community solar, including pollinator-friendly habitats or additional consideration for the impact potential on animals. We are currently performing energy audits in Japan, LED upgrades across UK facilities, remaining non-LED German shops, better monitoring, double doors in the new Glasgow store to improve efficiency, and ‘power down’ campaigns for staff.

Evolving Principles These are the values or principles we regard as so core to our philosophy that they will never be changed without proper consideration and consultation with all Lush Group Employees, so their views are taken into account when considering what action to take in relation to that proposed change. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 36 Principle: A Living Planet Evolving Principle: Lush believes it’s a part of the natural world, therefore its impact – be it through the ingredients we use, the way we manufacture and transport goods or how we generate energy – must give back more than it takes. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 37 PRINCIPLE: A LIVING PLANET Where we have the biggest impact on the living world is through our supply web. For our business to thrive - any business, really - we depend on clean water, healthy pollinators and abundant flora and fauna. Here is how we look at having a positive impact on nature. See the Renewable Energy, Environmental Policies and Recycled Materials Principles for more. Improving Farming by value are minimally processed natural materials, essential oils and absolutes. About half of that, our top Of all the raw natural materials, like cocoa and materials we buy, almonds, and essential oil plants like 62% lavender and rose, are estimated to take up 5,255 hectares of land.

text: We encourage suppliers to test for safety using non-animal testing methods and to fund the development of non-animal test methods. Every year, we will request all our suppliers, globally, to complete our Non Animal Testing (NAT) declaration form to ensure they are still compliant within our policy. In an ever changing supply market, constant vigilance is needed to ensure we replace any suppliers who, through changes in company behaviour or company ownership, suddenly fall outside of our compliance criteria. In these instances our buying team will move that supplier to our non-compliant list and will search for a different supplier of those ingredients. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 9 PRINCIPLE: ANIMAL TESTING We are also audited by Ethical Consumer every year, to ensure that we are complying with our own NAT policies. We also fund the Lush Prize - a biannual prize, run in partnership with Ethical Consumer and an independent panel of scientist judges, rewarding scientists and those working to end animal testing. We will continue to use every tool we have to fight animal testing until it is completely eliminated worldwide. Between 2020 and 2024 our NAT global compliance* rate has increased by 6%, from 86% to 90% in our most recent audit. * Instances of non-compliance by suppliers include ; failure by supplier to complete or return Lush’s Non-Animal Testing Declaration Forms ; the identification of safety testing that contradicts Lush’s NAT policy ; the merger or acquisition of compliant suppliers by non-compliant ones.
